title: Why deleting a secret does not remove it
description: Removing a secret in a new commit leaves every prior version in history, so the value remains retrievable until history is rewritten and the key is rotated.
body: Use when reasoning about whether a removed secret is safe. Do not assume a follow-up commit erases a leaked value. Version control retains every committed version, so a secret deleted in HEAD is still present in earlier commits and anyone with the repo can read it; only history rewriting removes it, and even then any copy already cloned retains it. Inspect history with a full-history scan. Validation signals include a history scan still flagging the value after a surface deletion. Risk boundary: because exposure cannot be fully undone, rotation is the only reliable remedy, so always rotate regardless of scrubbing.
